make a dashed amount of money if you can only collect a few dollars and
start a chicken-farm. Jolly sound scheme, Bertie! Say you buy a
hen--call it one hen for the sake of argument. It lays an egg every day
of the week. You sell the eggs seven for twenty-five cents. Keep of hen
costs nothing. Profit practically twenty-five cents on every seven
eggs. Or look at it another way: Suppose you have a dozen hens. Each of
the hens has a dozen chickens. The chickens grow up and have more
chickens. Why, in no time you'd have the place covered knee-deep in
hens, all laying eggs, at twenty-five cents for every seven. You'd make
a fortune. Jolly life, too, keeping hens!

high-born master was taken by surprise. He was felled. But before he
lost consciousness he perceived a young man in evening dress, wearing

assassin in combat, and my high-born master remembers no more. His

gratitude is princely. He seeks for this young man to reward him. Ah,
you should be proud of your brother, sir!

<narration>Oakes asked.

<narration>was the verdict, and I don't blame him. I don't see how it could have been murder. The door was locked on the inside, so nobody could have got in."

<human>Cobra!
<jeeves>Just so. In a Southampton boarding-house, in a room with a locked

the limpid simplicity of the affair, when the door was opened there was
no sign of any cobra. It couldn't have got out through the door,
because the door was locked. It couldn't have got out of the window,
because the window was too high up, and snakes can't jump. And it
couldn't have gotten up the chimney, because there was no chimney. So
there you have it.

<narration>Oakes laughed shortly. The idea amused him.

<narration>said Mr. Snyder in the benevolently paternal manner which had made a score of criminals refuse to believe him a detective until the moment when the handcuffs snapped on their wrists.

<human>That difficulty may be overcome. I would be happy to select books for
Mr. Little to read. Perhaps I might explain my idea further?
<jeeves>I can't say I quite grasp it yet.
<human>The method which I advocate is what, I believe, the advertisers call
Direct Suggestion, sir, consisting as it does of driving an idea home
by constant repetition. You may have had experience of the system?
<jeeves>You mean they keep on telling you that some soap or other is the best,
and after a bit you come under the influence and charge round the
corner and buy a cake?
<human>Exactly, sir. The same method was the basis of all the most valuable
propaganda during the recent war. I see no reason why it should not be
adopted to bring about the desired result with regard to the subject's
views on class distinctions. If young Mr. Little were to read day after
day to his uncle a series of narratives in which marriage with young
persons of an inferior social status was held up as both feasible and
admirable, I fancy it would prepare the elder Mr. Little's mind for the

emotions having been stirred, you ought to be able to do the rest on
your head. It will be like leaping on to a moving bus.

<jeeves>Look here, I tell you what it--
<human>I was in a car with a man once. I said to him: 'What would happen if

said, 'I couldn't. One's the foot brake, and the other's the
accelerator.' 'But suppose you did?' I said. 'I wouldn't,' he said. 'Now
we'll let her rip.' So he stamped on the accelerator. Only it turned out
to be the foot brake after all, and we stopped dead, and skidded into a
ditch. The advice I give to every young man starting life is: 'Never
confuse the unusual and the impossible.' Take the present case. If you
had only realized the possibility of somebody someday collaring your
study, you might have thought out dozens of sound schemes for dealing
with the matter. As it is, you are unprepared. The thing comes on you as
a surprise. The cry goes round: 'Spiller has been taken unawares. He
cannot cope with the situation.'

were, about 'ar parse five, Jane--she's the worst of the two, always up
to it, she is--she got hold of my old hat and had it in bits before you

a mouse, and they got on the coffee-room table and ate half a cold
chicken what had been left there. So I says to myself, 'I'll have a game

consequently, had not time to fade. I remember thinking myself, at the
moment, that the shoe appeared to have a certain reddish tint. The
mistake....

<narration>_A LETTER with a POSTSCRIPT_ I Mr. Jeremy Garnet stood with his back to the empty grate--for the time was summer--watching with a jaundiced eye the removal of his breakfast things.
<jeeves>Mrs. Medley,
<narration>he said.
<human>Sir?
<jeeves>Would it bore you if I became auto-biographical?
<human>Sir?
<jeeves>Never mind. I merely wish to sketch for your benefit a portion of my
life's history. At eleven o'clock last night I went to bed, and at
once sank into a dreamless sleep. About four hours later there was a
clattering on the stairs which shook the house like a jelly. It was
the gentleman in the top room--I forget his name--returning to roost.
He was humming a patriotic song. A little while later there were a
couple of loud crashes. He had removed his boots. All this while
snatches of the patriotic song came to me through the ceiling of my
bedroom. At about four-thirty there was a lull, and I managed to get
to sleep again. I wish when you see that gentleman, Mrs. Medley, you
would give him my compliments, and ask him if he could shorten his
program another night. He might cut out the song, for a start.

<human>I won't,
<narration>he muttered fiercely,
<jeeves>I will work. I'll turn out
something, even if it's the worst rot ever written.
<human>Oi'll--er--sing thee saw-ongs

<jeeves>ovarraby.

costs nothing. Profit at least fourpence, three farthings on every
half-dozen eggs. What do you think of that, Bartholomew?
<narration>Garnet admitted that it sounded like an attractive scheme, but expressed a wish to overhaul the figures in case of error.
<human>Error!
<narration>shouted Ukridge, pounding the table with such energy that it groaned beneath him.
<jeeves>Error? Not a bit of it. Can't you follow a

let you have a hen on tick. Now listen to me for a moment. You let
your hen set, and hatch chickens. Suppose you have a dozen hens. Very
well, then. When each of the dozen has a dozen chickens, you send the
old hens back with thanks for the kind loan, and there you are,
starting business with a hundred and forty-four free chickens to your
name. And after a bit, when the chickens grow up and begin to lay, all
you have to do is to sit back in your chair and gather in the big
checks. Isn't that so, Millie?

straight in. They can't eat you. It makes me sick when I see all those
poor devils hanging about outside these offices, waiting to get
noticed and nobody ever paying any attention to them. You push the
office-boy in the face if he tries to stop you, and go in and make 'em
take notice. And, whatever you do, don't leave your name and address!
That's the old, moth-eaten gag they're sure to try to pull on you.

Mason and George Bevan. They want a certain polish.... It was just the
same in Gilbert and Sullivan's day. They started writing at a time
when the musical stage had reached a terrible depth of inanity. The
theatre was given over to burlesques of the most idiotic description.
The public was waiting eagerly to welcome something of a higher class.
It is just the same to-day. But the managers will not see it. 'The
Rose of America' went up and down Broadway for months, knocking at
managers' doors.

Living in a little world of sycophants, he was galled by the off-hand
way in which Wally always treated him. There was something in the
latter's manner which seemed to him sometimes almost contemptuous. He
regretted the necessity of having to employ him. There was, of course,
no real necessity why he should have employed Wally. New York was full
of librettists who would have done the work equally well for half the
money, but, like most managers, Mr. Goble had the mental processes of
a sheep.

<narration>said Webster, pursuing a not uninteresting train of thought,

<jeeves>Why, you do know something about it after all!

<narration>said Sam, warming up. This he fancied, was the way Widgery would have tackled a troublesome client.

<narration>said Mr. Bennett.

<jeeves>Don't laugh at me!
<narration>roared Mr. Bennett.

<human>I'm not! I'm smiling quietly.

<narration>said Sam.

<human>I was reading a story--one of the Nosegay Novelettes; I do not know if

<narration>The heroine, Lady Blanche Trefusis, forced by her parents to wed a wealthy suitor, despatches a note to her humble lover, informing him it cannot be. I believe it often happens like that, sir."

<narration>said Sam.
<jeeves>It's not that at all.

<jeeves>Nothing like it! I--I----.

<human>At least, there was, but

<human>Well, it was like this.
<jeeves>All for Her,
<narration>where the hero, anxious to win the esteem of the lady of his heart, had bribed a tramp to simulate an attack upon her in a lonely road.
<human>The principle's the same,
<narration>said Webster.
<jeeves>Well, what did he do when she found out?
<human>She did not find out, sir. All ended happily, and never had the

<narration>Sam was thoughtful.
<jeeves>Bribed a tramp to attack her, did he?
<human>Yes, sir. She had never thought much of him till that moment, sir.

considerably inferior to her own. But, when she cried for help, and he
dashed out from behind a hedge, well, it made all the difference.
<jeeves>I wonder where I could get a good tramp,
<narration>said Sam, meditatively. Webster shook his head.
<human>I really would hardly recommend such a procedure, sir.
<jeeves>No, it would be difficult to make a tramp understand what you wanted.

<human>I've got it! _You_ pretend to attack her, and I'll....

<narration>...."

<narration>said Webster, a little hastily--flattered at the readiness with which his disciple was taking his advice, yet acutely alive to the fact that he slept at the top of the house himself.
<human>A little drastic, if I may say so. It might be

<human>Well, there is a lake in the grounds....
<jeeves>Excellent!

<jeeves>Three years ago, as you are aware, I was compelled to expunge from

<jeeves>But, Stanley, why shouldn't Aunt Elizabeth go to the Pen and Ink

<human>There is just one scheme. It occurred to me as I was sprinting

<narration>he proceeded, laying a heavy hand on my shoulder,

<jeeves>Mr. Corcoran is so clever. I'm sure, if it's anything that can be

<human>And one of the things she told me,
<narration>continued Ukridge,
<jeeves>was that
my aunt, though she's always speaking at these bally dinners, can't

it. Miss Mason swore solemnly to me that she had written every word
my aunt had spoken in public in the last two years. You begin to
get on to the scheme, laddie? The long and the short of it is that
we must get hold of that speech she's going to deliver at the Pen
and Ink Club binge. We must intercept it, old horse, before it can
reach her. We shall thus spike her guns. Collar that speech, Corky,
old man, before she can get her hooks on it, and you can take it
from me that she'll find she has a headache on Friday night and
can't appear.

<human>jaw
<narration>was inevitable, and he had been ready to go through with it. It might hurt his feelings, possibly, but it would leave his purse intact. A ghastly development of this kind he had not foreseen.

<narration>This struck him as a good beginning--modest but not grovelling. He continued, tapping quite a respectably deep vein of philosophy as he spoke.
<jeeves>You see, dear old top--I mean sir--you see, it's like this. As far as

<narration>--his voice grew soft and dreamy--

<human>That's right,
<narration>said the detective--

<narration>He laughed amusedly at the recollection.

<narration>said the detective.
<jeeves>Mighty ingenious idea, that,

Section  3

At about the moment when Sam entered the luxuriously furnished office of
the Mammoth Publishing Company's proprietor and chief, in a smaller and
less ornate room in the same building Mr. Matthew Wrenn, all unconscious
of the good fortune about to descend upon him in the shape of the
addition to his staff of a live and go-ahead young assistant, was seated
at his desk, busily engaged in promoting the best interests of that
widely read weekly, Pyke's _Home Companion_. He was, in fact, correcting
the proofs of an article--ably written, but too long to quote
here--entitled What a Young Girl Can Do in Her Spare Time; Number 3, Bee
Keeping.

He was interrupted in this task by the opening of the door, and looking
up, was surprised to see his niece, Kay Derrick.

<jeeves>Did you want anything?
<narration>he asked.
<human>What?
<narration>said Sam.
<jeeves>Is there anything I can do for you?
<human>Eh?
<narration>Mr. Wrenn approached the matter from a fresh angle.
<jeeves>This is the office of Pyke's _Home Companion_. I am Mr. Wrenn, the
editor. Did you wish to see me?
<human>Who?
<jeeves>I beg your pardon?
<human>You wished to see me?
<jeeves>Yes,
<narration>said Sam;
<human>yes, yes.
<jeeves>What about?
<narration>asked Mr. Wrenn patiently. The directness and simplicity of the question seemed to clear Sam's head. He recalled now what it was that had brought him here.
<human>I've come over from America to join the staff of Pyke's _Home
Companion_.
<jeeves>What?
<human>Lord Tilbury wants me to.
<jeeves>Lord Tilbury?
<human>Yes; I've just been seeing him.
<jeeves>But he has said nothing to me about this, Mr.----
<human>----Shotter. No, we only arranged it a moment ago.
<narration>Mr. Wrenn was a courteous man, and though he was under the impression that his visitor was raving, he did not show it.
<jeeves:thoughtful>Perhaps I had better see Lord Tilbury,
<narration>he suggested, rising.
<human>By the
way, my niece, Miss Derrick. Kay, my dear, Mr. Shotter.
<jeeves>Did you say you had come from America?
<narration>she asked, fiddling with Mr. Wrenn's editorial pencil. She had no desire to know, but she supposed she must engage this person in conversation.
<human>From America, yes. Yes, from America.
<jeeves>Is this your first visit to England?
<narration>asked Kay, stifling a yawn.
<human>Oh, no. I was at school in England.
<jeeves>Really? Where?
<human>At Wrykyn.
<narration>Kay's attitude of stiff aloofness relaxed. She became interested.
<jeeves>Good gracious! Of course!
<narration>She looked upon him quite benevolently.

<jeeves>What a splendid chap!
<human>Yes, he's a dear.
<jeeves>What a corker!
<human>Yes!
<jeeves>What an egg!
<human>Yes. Tell me, Mr. Shotter,
<narration>said Kay wearying of this eulogy,
<jeeves>do you
remember a boy at your school named Bates?
<narration>Sam's face darkened. Time had softened the anguish of that moment outside the Angry Cheese, but the sting still remained.
<human>Yes, I do.
<jeeves>Willoughby Braddock told me that you once beat Bates with a walking

<human>Yes.
<jeeves>A large walking stick?
<human>Yes.
<jeeves>Did you beat him hard?
<human>Yes, as hard as ever I could lay it in.
<narration>A little sigh of gratification escaped Kay.
<jeeves>Ah!

<human>It is quite all right, Mr. Shotter. Lord Tilbury wishes you to start

<narration>said Sam.

<narration>said Sam dully. Mr. Wrenn watched him shamble out of the room with a benevolent eye.

<narration>he said, removing the alpaca coat which it was his custom to wear in the office.
<human>Haven't had

<narration>he said.

<narration>said Kay. Mr. Wrenn, startled, dropped his hat.
<human>Eh? What do you mean?
<jeeves>Just what I say. He's horrible.
<human>But, my dear girl, you only met him five minutes ago.
<jeeves>I know.

<narration>he said.

<human>Mon Repos!

<human>Yes; and it is so difficult to avoid one's next-door neighbours.
<narration>Kay's teeth met with a little click.
<jeeves>It can be done,

<human>Why, hello, Dolly,
<narration>said Mr. Twist.
<jeeves>Hello,
<narration>said the girl moodily.
<human>Haven't seen you for a year, Dolly. Never knew you were this side at

<narration>The visitor took a seat.

<jeeves>Looks good, don't you think?
<narration>he said, stroking his moustache fondly. It and money were the only things he loved.
<human>Anything you say. And I suppose, when you know you may be in the coop

<jeeves>What's all this?
<jeeves>Just camouflage,
<narration>he said.
<human>Darned useful to have a label. Keeps people
from asking questions.
<jeeves>It won't keep me from asking questions. That's what I've come for. Say,

<human>You know me, Dolly.
<jeeves>Yes, that's why I asked. Well, I've come to get you to tell me

<human>Not a soul.
<jeeves>How about the office boy?
<human>I haven't got an office boy. Who do you think I am--Pierpont Morgan?
<narration>Thus reassured, the girl produced a delicate handkerchief, formerly the property of Harrod's Stores and parted from unwittingly by that establishment.
<jeeves>Chimp,
<narration>she said, brushing away a tear,
<human>I'm sim'ly miserable.
<narration>Chimp Twist was not the man to stand idly by while beauty in distress wept before him. He slid up and was placing a tender arm about her shoulder, when she jerked herself away.
<jeeves>You can tie a can to that stuff,
<narration>she said with womanly dignity.
<human>I'd

<jeeves>Married?
<human>Sure. Day before yesterday--to Soapy Molloy.
<jeeves>Soapy!
<narration>Mr. Twist started.
<human>What in the world did you want to marry

<jeeves>I'll ask you kindly, if you wouldn't mind,
<narration>said the girl in a cold voice,
<human>not to go alluding to my husband as slabs of Gorgonzola.
<jeeves>He is a slab of Gorgonzola.
<human>He is not. Well, anyway, I'm hoping he's not. It's what I come here to

<narration>Mr. Twist's mind had returned to the perplexing matter of the marriage.
<jeeves>I don't get this,
<narration>he said.
<human>I saw Soapy a couple of weeks back and he

<jeeves>He hadn't then. We only run into each other ten days ago. I was walking
up the Haymarket and I catch sight of a feller behind me out of the
corner of my eye, so I faint on him, see?
<human>You're still in that line, eh?
<jeeves>Well, it's what I do best, isn't it?
<human>Well, then I seen it was Soapy, and so we go to lunch and have a nice
chat. I always was strong for that boy, and we were both feeling kind of
lonesome over here in London, so we fix it up. And now I'm sim'ly
miserable.
<jeeves>What,
<narration>inquired Mr. Twist,
<human>is biting you?
<jeeves>Well, I'll tell you. This is what's happened: Last night this bird
Soapy goes out after supper and doesn't blow in again till four in the
morning. Four in the morning, I'll trouble you, and us only married two
days. Well, if he thinks a young bride's going to stand for that sort of
conduct right plumb spang in the middle of what you might call the
honeymoon, he's got a second guess due him.
<human>What did you do?
<narration>asked Mr. Twist sympathetically, but with a touch of that rather unctuous complacency which bachelors display at moments like this.
<jeeves>I did plenty. And he tried to alibi himself by pulling a story. That
story the grand jury is now going to investigate and investigate
good.... Chimp, did you ever hear of a man named Finglass?
<narration>There was that in Mr. Twist's manner that seemed to suggest that he was a reluctant witness, but he answered after a brief hesitation.
<human>Sure!
<jeeves>Oh, you did, eh? Well, who was he then?
<human>He was big,
<narration>said Chimp, and there was a note of reverence in his voice.
<jeeves>One of the very biggest, old Finky was.
<human>How was he big? What did he ever do?
<jeeves>Well, it was before your time and it happened over here, so I guess you
may not have heard of it; but he took a couple of million dollars away
from the New Asiatic Bank.
<narration>Mrs. Molloy was undeniably impressed. The formidable severity of her manner seemed to waver.
<human>Were you and Soapy mixed up with him?
<jeeves>Sure! We were the best pals he had.
<human>Is he alive?
<jeeves>No; he died in Buenos Aires the other day.
<narration>Mrs. Molloy bit her lower lip thoughtfully.
<human>Say, it's beginning to look to me like that story of Soapy's was the
goods after all. Listen, Chimp, I'd best tell you the whole thing. When
I give Soapy the razz for staying out all night like the way he done, he
pulled this long spiel about having had a letter from a guy he used to
know named Finglass, written on his deathbed, saying that this guy
Finglass hadn't been able to get away with the money he'd swiped from
this New Asiatic Bank on account the bulls being after him, and he'd had
to leave the whole entire lot of it behind, hidden in some house down in
the suburbs somewheres. And he told Soapy where the house was, and Soapy
claims that what kep' him out so late was he'd been searching the house,
trying to locate the stuff. And what I want to know is, was he telling
the truth or was he off somewheres at one of these here now gilded
night-clubs, cutting up with a bunch of janes and doing me wrong?

<narration>Again Mr. Twist seemed to resent the necessity of acting as a favourable witness for a man he obviously disliked. He struggled with his feelings for a space.
<jeeves>Yes, it's true,
<narration>he said at length.
<human>But listen here. This don't seem to me to gee up. If this guy Finglass
wanted Soapy to have the money, why did he wait all this time before
telling him about it?
<jeeves>Thought he might find a chance of sneaking back and getting it himself,
of course. But he got into trouble in Argentina almost as soon as he hit
the place, and they stowed him away in the cooler; and he only got out
in time to write the letters and then make his finish.
<human>How do you know all that?
<jeeves>Finky wrote to me too.
<human>Oh, did he? Well, then, here's another thing that don't seem to make
sense: When he did finally get round to telling Soap about this money,

leaving him hunt for it like he was playing button, button, where's the
button--or something?
<jeeves>Because,
<narration>said Mr. Twist bitterly,
<human>Soapy and me were both pals of his,
and he wanted us to share. And to make sure we should get together he

house.
<jeeves>So you've only to pool your info' to bring home the bacon?
<narration>cried Dolly, wide-eyed.
<human>That's all.
<jeeves>Then why in time haven't you done it?
<jeeves>Because,
<narration>he said,
<human>Soapy, the pig-headed stiff, thinks he can

<jeeves>What?
<narration>Mrs. Molloy uttered a cry of wifely pride.
<human>Well, isn't that
bright of my sweet old pieface! I'd never of thought the dear boy would

<narration>Mr. Twist was unable to share her pretty enthusiasm.
<jeeves>A lot it's going to get him!
<narration>he said sourly.

<narration>retorted Dolly.
<jeeves>Two million smackers nothing! The stuff's hidden in a place where he'd

<narration>The new-comer had had the air of a man weighed down with the maximum amount of sorrow which a human being can bear. This demonstration, however, seemed to remove something of the burden.
<jeeves>'S all right, sweetness,
<narration>he said, clasping her to his swelling bosom.

<human>Ah, cut it out!
<narration>he growled.

<narration>proceeded Mrs. Molloy.
<human>I know

<narration>he said, shaking his head.

<jeeves>The house has been taken,
<narration>said Soapy despondently.

<narration>he said nastily.

<narration>cried Mrs. Molloy.

<narration>said Chimp carelessly. He scribbled a few words on a piece of paper and covered them with his hand.
<human>There! Now you write

<narration>asked Mr. Molloy humbly.
<human>I've thought up a dozen.

<jeeves>In the cistern!
<narration>she read.
<human>And the rest of it?
<narration>inquired Mr. Twist pressingly.
<jeeves>Mon Repos, Burberry Road,
<narration>said Mr. Molloy.
<human>Ah!
<narration>said Chimp.
<jeeves>And if I'd known that a week ago, we'd have been

<narration>said Dolly sharply.

<narration>said Chimp.

<jeeves>Me and the madam counting as one, of course.

<narration>demanded Dolly,

<narration>retorted Mr. Twist with spirit.

<narration>he'd say----" Dolly wearied of a discussion which seemed to her too academic for the waste of valuable moments.

<narration>she said brusquely.
<jeeves>Seventy-thirty,
<narration>emended Chimp.
<human>Sixty-five-thirty-five,
<narration>said Mr. Molloy.
<jeeves>Right!
<narration>said Chimp.
<human>And now I'll tell you what to do.
<narration>I'll give you five minutes first to see if you can think of it for yourself, and if you can't, I'll ask you not to start beefing because it's so simple and not worth the money.
<jeeves>Five minutes' concentrated meditation produced no brain wave in Mr.
Molloy, who, outside his chosen profession of selling valueless oil
stock to a trusting public, was not a very gifted man.
<narration>Well, then,
<human>said Chimp,
<narration>Well, of all the fool propositions!
<jeeves>cried Dolly shrilly, and even Mr.
Molloy came near to sneering.
<narration>Not so good, you don't think?
<human>continued Chimp, uncrushed.
<narration>Am I her father?
<jeeves>asked Mr. Molloy, a little blankly.
<narration>Sure, you're her father. Why not?
<human>Mr. Molloy, who was a little sensitive about the difference in age

<narration>Then what?
<jeeves>asked Dolly.
<narration>Then,
<human>said Chimp,
<narration>Soapy does a spiel.

<human>said Mr. Molloy, starting.
<narration>Ages and ages ago,
<jeeves>repeated Chimp firmly,

<narration>that if that guy has any sentiment in him and if Dolly has done the preliminary work properly, he'll drop.
<jeeves>There was a tense silence.
<narration>It'll work,
<human>said Soapy.
<narration>It might work,
<jeeves>said Dolly, more doubtfully.

<human>said Soapy.

<jeeves>Chimp reminded him.

<human>said Chimp.
<narration>Eighteen guineas!
<jeeves>said Soapy.

They looked at each other wanly, while Dolly, unheeded, spoke of ships

<human>she continued, warming to her work.

<narration>A girl,
<jeeves>she said,
<narration>Oh, all right, all right,
<human>said Chimp.
<narration>All right,
<jeeves>echoed Mr. Molloy.

Their faces were set grimly. These men were brave, but they were
suffering.

CHAPTER FOURTEEN

THE CHIRRUP

Mr. Wrenn looked up from his plate with a sudden start, a wild and
febrile glare of horror in his eyes. Old theatregoers, had any such been
present, would have been irresistibly reminded by his demeanour of the
late Sir Henry Irving in _The Bells_.

It was breakfast time at San Rafael; and, as always at this meal, the
air was charged with an electric unrest. It is ever thus at breakfast in
the suburbs. The specter of a fleeting train broods over the feast,
turning normally placid men into temporary neuropaths. Meeting Mr. Wrenn
in Fleet Street after lunch, you would have set him down as a very
pleasant, quiet, elderly gentleman, rather on the mild side. At
breakfast, Bengal tigers could have picked up hints from him.

<human>Does a guy named Shotter live here?
<narration>asked the visitor. Then, with the air of one remembering a part and with almost excessive refinement,
<jeeves>Could I see Mr. Shotter, if you please?
<human>Mr. Shotter lives next door,
<narration>said Kay frostily.
<jeeves>Oh, thank yaw. Thank yaw so much.
<human>Not at all,
<jeeves>Claire,
<narration>she called.
<human>'Ullo!
<narration>replied a distant voice.
<jeeves>I'm leaving a note on the hall table. Will you take it next door some
time?
<human>Right-ho!
<narration>bellowed the obliging Miss Lippett. CHAPTER FIFTEEN VISITORS AT MON REPOS Sam was preparing to leave for the office when his visitor arrived. He had, indeed, actually opened the front door.
<jeeves>Mr. Shottah?
<human>Yes,
<jeeves>Won't you come in?

<jeeves>Cornelius?
<human>Gink with a full set of white whiskers. Say, somebody ought to put that
baby wise about the wonderful invention of the safety razor.

<jeeves>I'm afraid you'll find the place in rather a mess,
<narration>he said apologetically, leading the way to the drawing-room.

moved in.

<narration>she said.
<human>I've heard so much
about it from old pop.

<human>My father, I mean. He used to live here when he was a tiny kiddy.
<jeeves>Really? I should have taken you for an American.
<human>I am American, and don't let anyone tell you different.
<jeeves>I won't.
<human>One hundred per cent, that's me,
<narration>Sam nodded.

<human>It

<narration>he said.

<human>You want what?
<narration>said Sam, bewildered. He had had no notion that he was going to be swept into the maelstrom of a business transaction.
<jeeves>Yes, sir, I want this house. And let me tell you that money is no

<narration>He dismissed money with a gesture.
<human>I have
my whims and I can pay for them. How much for the house, Mr. Shotter?

<narration>he said.

When I was prospecting in Nevada, the boys used to call me Hair-Trigger

window and halted, spellbound.

take her hand. He saw Sam smile. He saw Sam hold her hand. And then it
seemed to him that he had seen enough. Abandoning his intention of

<human>I want to get to the bottom of this. What makes
you think that you and I were not designed for each other from the
beginning of time? I've been thinking very deeply about the whole thing,
and it beats me why you can't see it. To start with, we are so much
alike, we have the same tastes----
<jeeves>Have we?
<human>Most certainly. To take a single instance, we both dislike Claude
Bates. Then there is your love, which I share, for a life in the
country. The birds, the breezes, the trees, the bees--you love them and
so do I. It is my one ambition to amass enough money to enable me to buy
a farm and settle down. You would like that.
<jeeves>You seem to know a lot about me.
<human>I have my information from your uncle.
<jeeves>Don't you and uncle ever do any work at the office? You seem to spend
your whole time talking.
<human>In the process of getting together a paper like Pyke's _Home
Companion_, there come times when a little rest, a little folding of the
hands, is essential. Otherwise the machine would break down. On these
occasions we chat, and when we chat we naturally talk about you.
<jeeves>Why?
<human>Because there is no other subject in which I am in the least
interested. Well, then, returning to what I was saying, we are so much
alike----
<jeeves>They say that people should marry their opposites.
<human>Pyke's _Home Companion_ has exploded that view. Replying to Anxious

<human>I see. Rather serious. I must think this over.

What's that thing of Browning's that you're always quoting? Ah, yes!

'The morning's at seven;
The hillside's dew-pearled.
God's in his heaven;
All's right with the world.'

That is how I feel.

<narration>inquired Hash, picking up a derelict slice and holding it against the light as if it were some rare _objet d'art_. Sam perceived that his audience was not attuned to the lyrical note.

of the first men in this city to hiss the villain. Whether it was Tom
Mix who caused the trouble, or whether his weak intellect was gradually
sapped by seeing William H. Hart kiss his horse I cannot say: but the
fact remains that he now yearns for the great open spaces and, if you
want to ingratiate yourself with him, all you have to do is to mention

priceless a chump you may feel, and before many weeks are out you will

<narration>repeated the Sage.
<jeeves>One of those unfortunate beings who have
allowed this noblest of sports to get too great a grip upon them, who
have permitted it to eat into their souls, like some malignant growth.
The goof, you must understand, is not like you and me. He broods.
He becomes morbid. His goofery unfits him for the battles of life.
Jenkinson, for example, was once a man with a glowing future in the
hay, corn, and feed business, but a constant stream of hooks, tops,
and slices gradually made him so diffident and mistrustful of himself,
that he let opportunity after opportunity slip, with the result that
other, sterner, hay, corn, and feed merchants passed him in the race.
Every time he had the chance to carry through some big deal in hay, or
to execute some flashing _coup_ in corn and feed, the fatal diffidence
generated by a hundred rotten rounds would undo him. I understand his
bankruptcy may be expected at any moment.

There is none like her, none. I have been to pro. after pro., but not

a lack of sympathy and human understanding about these professionals
which jars on my artist soul. They look at you as if you were a
half-witted child. They click their tongues. They make odd Scotch
noises. I could not endure the strain. And then this wonderful girl,
to whom in a burst of emotion I had confided my unhappy case, offered
to give me private lessons. So I went with her to some of those indoor
practising places. But here, too, my sensibilities were racked by the
fact that unsympathetic eyes observed me. So I fixed up a room here
where we could be alone.
